architecture.summary: Backpack is a neural language model architecture where each vocabulary word is represented as a set of non-contextual sense vectors. For each token in a sequence, the model computes a context-dependent, non-negative linear combination of sense vectors (from all words in the sequence) weighted by a contextualization function (implemented as a Transformer). The final representation is passed through a log-linear output layer for prediction. This design ensures sense vectors contribute log-linearly to predictions, enabling interpretable intervention and inspection via projection onto the output space.
trained_components: contextualization network (Transformer that computes attention weights over sense vectors)

claims.statement: a 170M-parameter Backpack language model achieves OpenWebText perplexity matching that of a GPT-2 small (124M-parameter) Transformer
Backpack sense vectors outperform a 6B-parameter Transformer LM's word embeddings on lexical similarity evaluations

sense vector ablation: Mini-sized Backpack models with sense counts k=1,2,4,8 achieve OpenWebText perplexities of 38.6, 29.3, 26.0, and 24.1 respectively, demonstrating the necessity of a non-singleton set of sense vectors

gender bias reduction: removing sense 10 from profession words reduces the bias ratio from 4.34 to 2.88 (44% reduction); learning the optimal removal fraction per profession achieves a bias ratio of 2.16 (65% reduction)

sense vectors specialize after training, each encoding a different aspect of a word; individual senses encode coherent notions such as relatedness, next-token prediction, verb objects and noun modifiers, and proper noun associations

sense-controlled topic generation achieves at least as strong control as Plug-and-Play Language Models (PPLM) gradient-based control on a 124M Transformer baseline, evaluated across 17 topic categories

knowledge editing via sense vector nullspace projection and replacement allows localizing and editing associations with proper nouns (e.g., MacBook–Apple associations replaced with MacBook–HP associations) with minimal semantic or syntactic degradation

there is fundamental uncertainty whether Backpack language models will continue to scale with parameters and data and remain viable alternatives to Transformers at larger model scales; this study was unable to scale beyond 170M parameters
